articles.title: IMD Forecasts Thunderstorms, Unseasonal Rain In Maharashtra Amid Heatwave - Deccan Chronicle
articles.full_text:
IMD Forecasts Thunderstorms, Unseasonal Rain In Maharashtra Amid Heatwave
Authorities have urged residents to take necessary precautions, especially while travelling or engaging in outdoor activities, due to the risk posed by lightning and strong winds: Reports
MUMBAI: Along with intense heat, the India Meteorological Department (IMD has also indicated possible thunderstorms and unseasonal rain in some parts of Maharashtra starting Saturday.
As per the weather bureau, Latur, Nashik, Palghar, Raigad, Ratnagiri and Satara may experience light spells of rain and thunderstorms accompanied by lightning and gusty winds with speeds reaching 30-40 km/h in these regions.
Authorities have urged residents to take necessary precautions, especially while travelling or engaging in outdoor activities, due to the risk posed by lightning and strong winds.
The IMD has issued dry and heatwave alerts for areas including Dhule, Nashik, Nandurbar, Ahulyanagar, Satara, Sangli, Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar, Jalna, Nanded, Parbhani, and Hingoli. In Sangli, Solapur, Akola, Amravati, Buldhana and Yavatmal, the weather department has warned of heatwaves in isolated pockets, with a possibility of light to moderate rain accompanied by thunderstorms in the afternoon or evening.
According to IMD, the intensity of the heat is likely to persist over the next few days. Several districts, particularly in Vidarbha and Marathwada regions have recorded over 42-degree Celsius temperature for the entire week.
“It is imperative that citizens avoid venturing outdoors during the afternoon hours, drink plenty of water and seek immediate medical assistance at the first sign of symptoms,” the officials said.
While the state has been experiencing heatwave conditions for the last few weeks, the spell of rain over the weekend may make the conditions a bit cooler.
In Mumbai too, the Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC) urged residents to take precautions amid rising temperatures and humidity, cautioning about potential discomfort and health risks.

articles.title: Maharashtra: IMD forecasts heatwave-like conditions in Vidarbha and Marathwada tomorrow - News On AIR
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Maharashtra
articles.full_text:
In Maharashtra, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast heatwave-like conditions in isolated pockets of the Vidarbha region and Marathwada tomorrow.
The IMD has also predicted thunderstorms accompanied by lightning and light to moderate rainfall at isolated places in Vidarbha, North Maharashtra, and Marathwada till the 21st of this month.
A yellow alert has been issued for heatwave-like conditions in Jalgaon, Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar, Parbhani, Hingoli, Nanded, Amravati, Akola, Nagpur, Wardha, Washim, and Yavatmal districts tomorrow.
Further, the IMD has forecast thunderstorms with lightning, light to moderate rainfall, and gusty winds of upto 30-40 kmph in parts of Vidarbha, including Wardha, Yavatmal, Gadchiroli, Chandrapur, Akola, and Amravati, on 20th and 21st April.
The department has also predicted rainfall at isolated places in Western Maharashtra, including Pune, Kolhapur, and Satara, between the 19th and 21st of April.
Additionally, light to moderate rainfall is expected in isolated pockets of Solapur, Parbhani, Hingoli, Nanded, Latur, and Dharashiv districts.

articles.title: Heatwave alert in Maharashtra till April 20; Akola hottest at 44.2°C - The Hindu
articles.search_term: heatwave (Ahmednagar OR Akola OR Amravati OR Aurangabad OR Beed OR Bhandara OR Buldhana OR Chandrapur OR Dhule OR Gadchiroli OR Gondia OR Hingoli OR Jalgaon OR Jalna OR Kolhapur OR Latur OR "Mumbai City" OR "Mumbai Suburban" OR Nagpur OR Nanded OR Nandurbar OR Nashik OR Osmanabad OR Palghar OR Parbhani OR Pune OR Raigad OR Ratnagiri OR Sangli OR Satara OR Sindhudurg OR Solapur OR Thane OR Wardha OR Washim OR Yavatmal) Maharashtra
articles.full_text:
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a heatwave alert for six States, including Maharashtra, where the warning will remain in place for four days till Monday (April 20, 2026).
This year, Akola district has so far recorded the highest temperature in the country at 44.2°C, followed by Wardha, Amravati, and Nagpur districts.
According to IMD, there will be a gradual rise in maximum temperature by 1-2°C over parts of Konkan during the next three days, followed by a fall of 2-3°C thereafter. Regions including Palghar, Thane, Mumbai, Raigad, Ratnagiri, and Sindhudurg are expected to witness hot and humid conditions. Central Maharashtra districts of Dhule, Jalgaon, Nashik, Ahilya Nagar, Satara, Sangli, and Solapur may experience heatwave conditions, with maximum temperature likely to be in the range 42-44°C during the next three days and a gradual fall thereafter. A heatwave alert has also been issued for parts of Marathwada as well.
IMD officials attributed the rising temperatures to an anti-cyclonic circulation existing at a height of 3.1 km above the mean sea level over Maharashtra. Bikram Singh, chief scientist at the Regional Meteorological Centre, said, “One of the reasons for heat is rising pollutants around us. However, the IMD is vigilant and has already sent the alerts.”
“Mumbai Metropolitan Region (MMR) is developing but not sustainably; massive concrete projects absorb heat, making the climate warmer, said Dr Pradeep Awate, former State Surveillance Officer, Maharashtra Health Services, at an interactive session of the Regional Meteorological Centre (RMC) in Mumbai on Thursday (April 16).
Dr. Awate said, “Mumbai is a classic example of how heat waves are intensifying. The use of cement and concrete in several projects, along with a decline in the number of trees, is driving up the heat in Mumbai. This development is not sustainable, which is why we are experiencing increased heat.”
Citing studies, Mr. Awate highlighted that Mumbai’s temperature increased by 2% between 1991 and 2018. During the same period, construction activity went up by 66%, while open land declined by 81%, and this has been the trend since 2018.
The Maharashtra Government has rolled out standard operating procedures (SOPs) to address the heatwave impact. Mr. Awate pointed out that there is a need to identify the most vulnerable group, particularly outdoor labourers, and generate the SOPs accordingly.
The State has regulated working hours during heatwaves, limiting outdoor work between 12 noon and 4 p.m. But, activists said that the implementation of SOPs needs to be ensured, as people are still unaware of the health risks posed by extreme heat. The SOPs aim to reduce heat exposure, improve workplace safety, and ensure preparedness.
The other five States facing headwaves are Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Orissa, Jharkhand and Karnataka.
Published - April 17, 2026 05:39 pm IST
This is a premium article, available exclusively to subscribers.

articles.title: Nagpur Sizzles at 44°C Amid Intensifying Heatwave - The Live Nagpur
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Maharashtra
articles.full_text:
Vidarbha continued to reel under intense heatwave conditions on Friday, with temperatures soaring above 43°C across most districts. Notably, Nagpur recorded a scorching 44.0°C, reflecting the severe daytime heat gripping the region. This is the highest temperature the city has registered during the current summer season. The citizens are bearing the brunt increasingly of the scorching heat and preferring to remain at cool places
Wardha emerged as the hottest location at 44.4°C, followed by Akola at 44.1°C and Nagpur & Gadchiroli at 44°C.
The persistent high temperatures have raised concerns among residents, especially in urban centres like Nagpur, where heat exposure during peak afternoon hours has intensified. Authorities have advised people to avoid direct sunlight, stay hydrated, and take necessary precautions as heatwave conditions are likely to continue over the coming days.

articles.title: Heatwave Alert Across Maharashtra; Yellow Alert For Mumbai Amid Rising Temperatures, Health Risks - ETV Bharat
articles.search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Maharashtra
articles.full_text:
Heatwave Alert Across Maharashtra; Yellow Alert For Mumbai Amid Rising Temperatures, Health Risks
Maharashtra faces heatwave and humidity alerts with soaring temperatures, health risks, and weakened monsoon due to El Niño; hospitals on alert.
Mumbai: The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued heatwave and humidity alerts across Maharashtra, including a yellow alert for Mumbai, with a spike in temperatures and weakened monsoon due to El Niño conditions raising health concerns.
In its ‘Heat Advisory', the Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC) urged residents to take precautions amid rising temperatures and humidity, cautioning about potential discomfort and health risks. Several hospitals have already reported an increase in patients seeking treatment for heat-related illnesses.
Dean of the government-run KEM Hospital, Dr Sangeeta Rawat, said that orders had been issued to all hospitals to remain on standby, and ambulance services, medical supplies, and emergency facilities have been made available at full capacity.
“We have also issued directives to enhance the provision of drinking water at public places across the city. There has been an increase of nearly 15 to 20 per cent of patients coming with complaints of heat-related ailments since the last few days,” she told ETV Bharat.
Dr Rawat emphasized the seriousness of rising temperatures, saying that heat strokes need to be taken seriously. She warned that ignoring symptoms such as headaches, dizziness, vomiting, and physical weakness could be life-threatening. “It is absolutely essential to seek timely medical treatment. Both young children, senior citizens, and patients with chronic illnesses face a higher risk of heat-related complications,” she added.
Other health experts have also cautioned people, asking them to cover their heads and protect themselves from this heat by carrying water, ORS or electrolyte powders.
People, too, are finding it difficult with the rising temperatures that have suddenly begun due to El Niño.
“It is impossible to step out of the house in the afternoons. The scorching heat is causing the flowers to wither prematurely, and we are forced to take extra precautions to preserve them,” said Jagannath Ingle, a flower vendor from Dadar.
Lata Shinde, a commuter who travels daily by local train from Thane, said, “It is indeed impossible to travel in the local trains. The stifling heat inside the local trains and buses has made my commute more arduous.”
The IMD has issued dry and heatwave alerts for areas including Dhule, Nashik, Nandurbar, Ahulyanagar, Satara, Sangli, Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar, Jalna, Nanded, Parbhani, and Hingoli. In Sangli, Solapur, Akola, Amravati, Buldhana, and Yavatmal, the weather department has warned of heatwaves in isolated pockets, with a possibility of light to moderate rain accompanied by thunderstorms in the afternoon or evening.
Meanwhile, Akola set a record on Thursday with the highest temperature in the country at 44.2 degrees Celsius. Other cities, such as Wardha, Amravati, and Nagpur, also reported temperatures significantly above historical norms.
In Nagpur the District administration declared a holiday for all schools on April 18 after the IMD issued yellow alert. "This notification has been issued to protect the well-being and health of all students. All Anganwadis, primary and middle-level schools, colleges and coaching classes in the Nagpur district have been asked to give a holiday on Saturday, April 18," distict collector said in a notification.
Preliminary reports have emerged of heat strokes in certain parts of the state on April 15 and 16. Local health authorities have reported there have been suspected cases of heatstroke in the rural areas of Akola and Yavatmal districts in the Vidarbha region, as well as in the Nagpur area.
In Akola, an auto driver was found dead, and it is suspected to be due to heatstroke. Investigations are underway. All district collectors are reported to have taken meetings to take measures to prevent heat strokes.
According to IMD, the intensity of the heat is likely to persist over the next few days.
“Therefore, it is imperative that citizens avoid venturing outdoors during the afternoon hours, drink plenty of water, and seek immediate medical assistance at the first sign of symptoms,” it said.
Doctors have advised people to avoid going out in the sun between 12 PM and 4 PM. Citizens have been advised to drink plenty of water, wear light and loose clothes, and use umbrellas or caps/hats to protect themselves.

articles.title: Maharashtra heatwave: Vidarbha records above 44 degrees Celsius, remains hottest region in state - Mid-day
articles.full_text:
Vidarbha remained significantly hotter than Marathwada on Friday. Pic/ Ashish Raje
Most parts of Maharashtra on Friday continued to witness intense hot weather conditions, with interior regions recording scorching temperatures, while coastal belts remained relatively cooler but humid.
According to the weather data, Wardha emerged as the hottest location in the state at 44.4 degrees Celsius, closely followed by Akola (44.1 degrees Celsius) and Nagpur (44.0 degrees Celsius).
In contrast, Nashik recorded the lowest minimum temperature at 17.2 degrees Celsius, highlighting a sharp contrast of over 27 degrees.
Vidarbha remained the worst-affected region, with several cities crossing the 43 degrees Celsius mark, including Chandrapur (43.6 degrees Celsius) and Bramhapuri (43.0 degrees Celsius).
In Madhya Maharashtra, cities like Jalgaon (42.3 degrees Celsius), Solapur (42.2 degrees Celsius), and Lohgaon (41.5 degrees Celsius) also reported high temperatures. Pune recorded a maximum of 40.3 degrees Celsius.
Meanwhile, the Konkan region, including Mumbai (33.0 degrees Celsius) and Alibag (32.9 degrees Celsius), remained relatively cooler due to coastal influence, though humidity levels stayed high.
No rainfall was recorded anywhere in Maharashtra over the past 24 hours, with clear skies prevailing across regions.
For Pune, the forecast for Saturday indicates mainly clear skies, with temperatures expected to hover around 39 degrees Celsius (maximum) and 19 degrees Celsius (minimum). Humidity levels dropped significantly through the day on Friday, from 50 per cent in the morning to 19 per cent by evening.
Despite the ongoing heat, Pune has recorded excess seasonal rainfall so far this year, with 92 millimetres against a normal of 7.1 millimetres.
Vidarbha remained significantly hotter than Marathwada on Friday, recording extreme maximum temperatures across districts. Wardha touched 44.4 degrees Celsius, followed by Nagpur at 44.0 degrees Celsius, Akola at 44.1 degrees Celsius, Chandrapur at 43.6 degrees Celsius, and Bramhapuri at 43.0 degrees Celsius.
Marathwada was also hot but comparatively milder. Beed recorded 41.9 degrees Celsius, Parbhani 41.8 degrees Celsius, and Dharashiv 41.0 degrees Celsius, with no station crossing the 42 degrees Celsius mark.
The data shows Vidarbha was consistently 2 to 3 degrees Celsius hotter than Marathwada across most locations, making it the worst-affected region in Maharashtra's ongoing early heatwave.

articles.title: Weather Alert Maharashtra: Heatwave Warning and Unseasonal Rain Forecast for April 18; Yellow Alerts Issued Across Districts - PUNE PULSE
articles.full_text:
Maharashtra weather update: IMD predicts heatwave conditions along with unseasonal rain in several districts on April 18. Yellow alerts issued for Vidarbha, Marathwada, and parts of Western Maharashtra.
Maharashtra, April 17: The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a dual weather alert for Maharashtra on April 18, forecasting a mix of heatwave conditions and unseasonal rainfall across various regions of the state.
According to the latest forecast, some districts are likely to witness cloudy skies with moderate rainfall, while others remain under a yellow alert for rising temperatures, indicating a challenging mix of weather conditions for residents.
Konkan Region
In the Konkan belt, Sindhudurg is expected to experience cloudy conditions with moderate rainfall. However, cities like Mumbai, Thane, Raigad, and Ratnagiri are likely to remain dry, with maximum temperatures ranging between 34°C and 36°C and minimum temperatures between 25°C and 27°C.
Western Maharashtra
Districts including Kolhapur, Satara, and Sangli may receive moderate rainfall under cloudy skies. Meanwhile, Pune, Solapur, and surrounding areas are expected to face intense heat, with Solapur also under a yellow alert for possible rainfall. Temperatures here may rise between 36°C and 39°C.
Marathwada
In Marathwada, Beed and Latur are likely to receive rainfall, while Dharashiv remains under a yellow alert for rain. On the other hand, districts such as Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar, Jalna, Parbhani, Hingoli, and Nanded are under a yellow alert for heatwave conditions, with temperatures likely to soar between 38°C and 41°C.
Vidarbha
The Vidarbha region is expected to bear the brunt of the heatwave. Districts including Akola, Amravati, Nagpur, Wardha, Washim, and Yavatmal are under a yellow alert, with temperatures expected to reach 40°C to 43°C. Heatwave conditions are likely in these areas.
Rest of Maharashtra
No specific alerts have been issued for some parts of the state; however, the weather is expected to remain hot and dry, with maximum temperatures ranging between 35°C and 38°C and minimum temperatures between 23°C and 26°C.
Advisory
Residents are advised to stay hydrated, avoid prolonged exposure to direct sunlight during peak hours, and remain cautious of sudden weather changes, especially in districts likely to experience rainfall and thunderstorms.
Disclaimer: This forecast is based on the latest update from the India Meteorological Department and may change depending on evolving weather conditions. Residents are advised to follow official IMD updates for real-time information.
